original_abstract:
This study examines the evolution of legal protection for investors in the Indonesian capital market, specifically focusing on the transition of supervisory authority from Bapepam-LK to the Financial Services Authority (OJK). Historically, Bapepam-LK operated under the Ministry of Finance and was accountable to the Minister, whereas the OJK is an independent body answerable to the public and Parliament. A primary driver behind the OJK’s establishment was the need to address deficiencies in protecting financial service consumers. 

This paper analyzes the legal frameworks governing investor rights, drawing on Law No. 8 of 1995 concerning Capital Markets and Law No. 21 of 2011 concerning the Financial Services Authority. Using a recent case study of system failures at the Indonesia Stock Exchange (IDX) that obstructed trading and transparency, the research explores how these two legal regimes define the rights of investors as consumers and the mechanisms available for both preventive and repressive legal protection. The findings highlight the importance of OJK’s role in balancing systemic stability with the protection of individual investor interests through education, supervision, and administrative enforcement.
humanized_abstract:
This paper discusses the development of Investor Protection in Indonesian Capital Market particularly through the shifting of market supervisor from Bapepam-LK (now Commodity Futures Regulatory Body) to OJK (Financial Services Authority). Bapepam-LK was a government agency which is controlled by the Minister of Finance. In contrast, OJK was formed as a government institution with autonomy over supervision, which held the OJK accountable to the people as well as to the legislative body, specifically to the parliament. One of the main reasons why the establishment of OJK is because of ineffective consumer protection in the financial services industry.

This paper aims to explain about the applicable regime of rights for investors, as is stipulated by Law No. 8 of 1995 on Capital Market and Law No. 21 of 2011 on Financial Services Authority (OJK). By combining a recent case study that the Indonesia Stock Exchange (IDX) was exposed to a system failure which interrupted the trading activities and undermined the disclosure of share prices, this research study elucidates in-depth the regulation of rights for investors as consumers as are illustrated in both legal frameworks on capital market and solutions of the preventive and applicative protection for investors, implemented by OJK so as to maintain the equilibrium between stability of the system and investor protection through informative materials, monitoring, and administrative sanction.
